questions.question: During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA replication occur?
choices: G1 phase of Interphase
S phase (Synthesis phase) of Interphase
G2 phase of Interphase
Prophase of Mitosis
questions.answer: S phase (Synthesis phase) of Interphase
questions.explanation: DNA replication occurs exclusively during the S (Synthesis) phase of interphase, between G1 (cell growth and preparation) and G2 (continued growth). During S phase, each chromosome is duplicated into two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere, ensuring each daughter cell receives a complete genome.

questions.question: In a dihybrid cross AaBb × AaBb (with independent assortment), what fraction of offspring will show BOTH recessive phenotypes?
questions.explanation: For independently assorting genes: P(aa) = 1/4 and P(bb) = 1/4. By the product rule, P(aabb) = 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/16. The classic dihybrid ratio is 9:3:3:1, where 1 out of 16 shows both recessive traits. This follows from Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment.

questions.question: During an action potential, the rapid depolarization phase (rising phase) is caused by:
choices: Rapid efflux of K⁺ ions through voltage-gated potassium channels
Rapid influx of Na⁺ ions through voltage-gated sodium channels
Influx of Ca²⁺ ions through ligand-gated calcium channels
Active pumping of Na⁺ out of the cell by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase
questions.answer: Rapid influx of Na⁺ ions through voltage-gated sodium channels
questions.explanation: When a neuron is depolarized to threshold (~−55 mV), voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open rapidly, allowing Na⁺ to rush INTO the cell down its electrochemical gradient. This influx of positive charge drives the membrane potential from ~−70 mV to approximately +30–40 mV. K⁺ efflux drives repolarization, not depolarization.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes a competitive inhibitor of an enzyme?
choices: It binds the allosteric site, decreasing Vmax without changing Km
It binds the active site, increasing Km without changing Vmax
It covalently modifies the active site, permanently abolishing enzyme activity
It binds the enzyme-substrate complex, decreasing both Km and Vmax
questions.answer: It binds the active site, increasing Km without changing Vmax
questions.explanation: A competitive inhibitor competes with the substrate for the active site. At high enough substrate concentrations, it can be outcompeted — so Vmax is unchanged. However, it takes more substrate to reach half-maximal velocity, so apparent Km increases. This inhibition is reversible, unlike covalent (irreversible) inhibition.

questions.question: According to the Ten Percent Rule (Lindeman's efficiency) in ecology, what percentage of energy is typically transferred from one trophic level to the next?
questions.explanation: Approximately 10% of the energy at one trophic level is available to the next. The remaining ~90% is lost as heat (cellular respiration), used for metabolism, or remains in indigestible matter. This explains why food chains are typically limited to 4–5 trophic levels and why plant-based diets are more energy-efficient.

questions.question: The Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium requires five conditions. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
choices: No mutation
Random mating
Large population size
High genetic recombination rate
questions.answer: High genetic recombination rate
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires: (1) no mutation, (2) random mating, (3) large population size (no genetic drift), (4) no gene flow (no migration), and (5) no natural selection. A high recombination rate is NOT a requirement — recombination reshuffles alleles but does not change their frequencies.

questions.question: A gene is transcribed into mRNA, but no protein product is detected in the cell. Which post-transcriptional mechanism BEST explains this?
choices: Promoter methylation silencing transcription initiation at the DNA level
MicroRNA (miRNA)-mediated translational repression or mRNA degradation
Histone acetylation increasing chromatin accessibility for transcription
Codon bias reducing the rate of DNA replication in that genomic region
questions.answer: MicroRNA (miRNA)-mediated translational repression or mRNA degradation
questions.explanation: miRNAs are ~22-nucleotide non-coding RNAs that bind complementary sequences in the 3' UTR of target mRNAs, causing translational repression or mRNA cleavage. Since the gene IS transcribed (mRNA present) but protein is absent, this is a post-transcriptional phenomenon — ruling out epigenetic silencing at the DNA level.

questions.question: Which plant hormone is primarily responsible for phototropism — the bending of plant shoots toward light?
choices: Cytokinin
Gibberellin
Auxin (Indole-3-acetic acid, IAA)
Abscisic acid (ABA)
questions.answer: Auxin (Indole-3-acetic acid, IAA)
questions.explanation: Phototropism is driven by lateral redistribution of auxin (IAA). When light strikes one side of a shoot, auxin moves to the shaded side, causing those cells to elongate more — bending the shoot toward the light. This is the Cholodny-Went theory of phototropism.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly distinguishes gram-positive from gram-negative bacteria?
choices: Gram-positive bacteria have an outer membrane; gram-negative bacteria do not
Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan wall and no outer membrane; gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane
Gram-positive bacteria stain pink; gram-negative bacteria stain purple after Gram staining
Gram-negative bacteria are more susceptible to penicillin than gram-positive bacteria
questions.answer: Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan wall and no outer membrane; gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane
questions.explanation: Gram-positive bacteria retain the crystal violet stain (appear purple) because their thick peptidoglycan layer traps the dye. Gram-negative bacteria lose it during decolorization (thin peptidoglycan + outer membrane with lipopolysaccharide) and stain pink with safranin counterstain. The outer membrane also makes gram-negative bacteria more resistant to many antibiotics.

questions.question: According to Carl Woese's Three-Domain System, which of the following is NOT one of the three domains of life?
choices: Bacteria
Archaea
Eukarya
Protista
questions.answer: Protista
questions.explanation: Woese proposed the Three-Domain System (1990) based on ribosomal RNA sequences: Bacteria (prokaryotes), Archaea (prokaryotes with archaeal biochemistry), and Eukarya (all eukaryotes — protists, fungi, plants, animals). Protista is a Kingdom within the domain Eukarya, not a domain itself.

questions.question: What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis?
choices: Mitosis produces 4 haploid cells; meiosis produces 2 diploid cells
Mitosis produces 2 genetically identical diploid cells for growth and repair; meiosis produces 4 genetically unique haploid gametes for sexual reproduction
Mitosis occurs in reproductive cells only; meiosis occurs in all body cells
Both processes are identical except for the number of divisions
questions.answer: Mitosis produces 2 genetically identical diploid cells for growth and repair; meiosis produces 4 genetically unique haploid gametes for sexual reproduction
questions.explanation: Mitosis: one division → 2 identical diploid daughter cells (2n). Used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction. Meiosis: two divisions → 4 haploid cells (n) that are genetically unique due to crossing over and independent assortment. Used to produce gametes in sexually reproducing organisms.

questions.question: What is the function of the mitochondria?
choices: Synthesizing proteins from mRNA instructions
Producing ATP (cellular energy) through aerobic cellular respiration
Packaging and secreting proteins for export from the cell
Breaking down cellular waste and foreign materials
questions.answer: Producing ATP (cellular energy) through aerobic cellular respiration
questions.explanation: The mitochondrion is the 'powerhouse of the cell' — it produces most of the cell's ATP through aerobic respiration. The inner mitochondrial membrane houses the electron transport chain and ATP synthase. The mitochondrion also has its own DNA, evidence for the endosymbiotic theory.

questions.question: What is the central dogma of molecular biology?
choices: Proteins → RNA → DNA (reverse information flow)
DNA → mRNA (transcription) → Protein (translation)
DNA → Protein (direct translation without RNA)
RNA → DNA → Protein (reverse transcription is universal)
questions.answer: DNA → mRNA (transcription) → Protein (translation)
questions.explanation: The central dogma (Crick, 1958) describes the flow of genetic information: DNA is transcribed into mRNA in the nucleus, then mRNA is translated into protein by ribosomes in the cytoplasm. Exceptions exist: retroviruses use reverse transcriptase to copy RNA back into DNA.

questions.question: What is the difference between a genotype and a phenotype?
choices: Genotype is observable; phenotype is genetic
Genotype is the genetic makeup (alleles) of an organism; phenotype is the observable physical expression of those genes (plus environment)
Genotype determines only physical traits; phenotype includes only behavioral traits
They are interchangeable terms in classical genetics
questions.answer: Genotype is the genetic makeup (alleles) of an organism; phenotype is the observable physical expression of those genes (plus environment)
questions.explanation: Genotype refers to the actual allele combinations an organism carries (e.g., Tt, AABB). Phenotype refers to the observable characteristics (tall plant, brown eyes) that result from genotype-environment interaction. Two organisms can have the same phenotype but different genotypes (e.g., TT and Tt both produce tall plants).

questions.question: What is natural selection and what are its requirements?
choices: Random changes in allele frequencies in a population over time
The process by which heritable traits that increase survival and reproduction become more common over generations, requiring variation, heritability, and differential reproduction
The deliberate selection of organisms with desired traits for breeding purposes
The elimination of all harmful mutations from a population over time
questions.answer: The process by which heritable traits that increase survival and reproduction become more common over generations, requiring variation, heritability, and differential reproduction
questions.explanation: Natural selection requires: (1) variation in traits among individuals, (2) heritability of those traits, and (3) differential survival and reproduction ('fitness'). Organisms whose heritable traits best suit the environment leave more offspring, so favorable traits become more common over generations (Darwin, 1859).

questions.question: What is osmosis?
choices: The movement of solutes from high to low concentration across a membrane
The movement of water molecules across a semipermeable membrane from a region of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration
Active transport of water molecules against a concentration gradient
The diffusion of gases across cell membranes during respiration
questions.answer: The movement of water molecules across a semipermeable membrane from a region of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane, driven by the osmotic gradient (water moves toward higher solute/lower water potential). Cells in hypotonic solutions swell; in hypertonic solutions, they shrink (crenate in animals, plasmolyze in plants).

questions.question: What is the role of decomposers in an ecosystem?
choices: Converting light energy into chemical energy for primary producers
Breaking down dead organic matter and recycling nutrients back into the ecosystem
Transferring energy from primary consumers to secondary consumers
Regulating population sizes of herbivores in a community
questions.answer: Breaking down dead organic matter and recycling nutrients back into the ecosystem
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and organic waste, releasing inorganic nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus, carbon) back into the soil and atmosphere. Without decomposers, nutrients would remain locked in dead matter and primary producers could not sustain themselves.

questions.question: What is the endosymbiotic theory?
choices: The theory that viruses evolved from symbiotic bacteria that invaded host cells
The theory that mitochondria and chloroplasts originated as free-living prokaryotes that were engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells
The theory that eukaryotes evolved directly from archaea without endosymbiosis
The theory that cells form symbiotic partnerships to increase metabolic efficiency
questions.answer: The theory that mitochondria and chloroplasts originated as free-living prokaryotes that were engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells
questions.explanation: The endosymbiotic theory (Margulis, 1967) proposes that mitochondria evolved from engulfed α-proteobacteria and chloroplasts from engulfed cyanobacteria. Evidence includes: both have their own circular DNA, double membranes, ribosomes similar to bacteria, and divide by binary fission.

questions.question: What is genetic drift and when does it have the greatest effect?
choices: Deliberate selection of alleles by humans in artificial breeding programs
Random changes in allele frequencies in a population due to chance events, greatest in small populations
The gradual accumulation of mutations in a population over many generations
The flow of genes between populations through migration
questions.answer: Random changes in allele frequencies in a population due to chance events, greatest in small populations
questions.explanation: Genetic drift is a mechanism of evolution — random sampling errors in reproduction that change allele frequencies unpredictably. In small populations, drift can be very powerful (Founder effect, Bottleneck effect), potentially fixing harmful alleles or eliminating beneficial ones by chance alone.

questions.question: What is the greenhouse effect and why is it important?
choices: The warming of greenhouses for plant cultivation in cold climates
The trapping of solar heat in Earth's atmosphere by greenhouse gases like CO₂, CH₄, and H₂O — essential for life but intensified by human activities
The reflection of solar radiation by Earth's surface back into space
The cooling effect produced by vegetation in urban environments
questions.answer: The trapping of solar heat in Earth's atmosphere by greenhouse gases like CO₂, CH₄, and H₂O — essential for life but intensified by human activities
questions.explanation: The natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth habitable (~15°C average instead of -18°C). Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation. The enhanced greenhouse effect from burning fossil fuels is the primary driver of global climate change.

questions.question: What is apoptosis?
choices: The uncontrolled division of cancer cells
Programmed cell death — a natural, regulated process of cellular self-destruction essential for development and homeostasis
Cell death caused by external injury or toxins
The process of cell differentiation during embryonic development
questions.answer: Programmed cell death — a natural, regulated process of cellular self-destruction essential for development and homeostasis
questions.explanation: Apoptosis is genetically programmed cell death that occurs normally — removing damaged cells, shaping organs during embryonic development (e.g., separating fingers), and eliminating immune cells after infection. Dysregulation of apoptosis is implicated in cancer (too little) and neurodegenerative diseases (too much).

questions.question: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
choices: Aerobic respiration occurs in mitochondria; anaerobic occurs in the nucleus
Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and produces up to 36-38 ATP per glucose; anaerobic occurs without oxygen and produces only 2 ATP
Aerobic respiration produces CO₂; anaerobic produces only water
Aerobic respiration is less efficient than anaerobic respiration
questions.answer: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and produces up to 36-38 ATP per glucose; anaerobic occurs without oxygen and produces only 2 ATP
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + 36-38 ATP (via glycolysis + Krebs cycle + ETC). Anaerobic (fermentation): glycolysis only → 2 ATP + lactate (animals) or ethanol + CO₂ (yeast). Aerobic is much more efficient but requires oxygen.

questions.question: What is CRISPR-Cas9 and its significance in biology?
choices: A virus that infects bacteria and alters their DNA
A precise gene editing tool derived from a bacterial immune system that can cut and modify DNA at specific target sequences
A protein that repairs DNA damage caused by radiation
A diagnostic tool for identifying genetic mutations in patients
questions.answer: A precise gene editing tool derived from a bacterial immune system that can cut and modify DNA at specific target sequences
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 (Doudna & Charpentier, Nobel Prize 2020) uses guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, where it cuts both strands. The cell's repair mechanisms can then be exploited to delete, correct, or insert genetic sequences, revolutionizing medicine, agriculture, and basic research.

questions.question: What is mutualism and provide an example?
choices: A relationship where one organism benefits and the other is harmed
A symbiotic relationship where both organisms benefit, e.g., mycorrhizae-plant mutualism or clownfish-sea anemone
A relationship where one organism benefits and the other is unaffected
Competition between two species for the same resource
questions.answer: A symbiotic relationship where both organisms benefit, e.g., mycorrhizae-plant mutualism or clownfish-sea anemone
questions.explanation: Mutualism is a symbiosis where both species benefit. Examples: (1) mycorrhizal fungi + plant roots (fungi get sugars, plant gets phosphorus), (2) clownfish + sea anemone (fish protected by stinging tentacles, fish droppings fertilize anemone), (3) nitrogen-fixing Rhizobium + legumes.

questions.question: What is homeostasis and give an example in humans?
choices: The process of cellular differentiation during development
The maintenance of a stable internal environment despite external changes, e.g., regulating blood glucose by insulin and glucagon
The evolutionary tendency of organisms to become more complex over time
The balanced state of an ecosystem when no species are added or removed
questions.answer: The maintenance of a stable internal environment despite external changes, e.g., regulating blood glucose by insulin and glucagon
questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the ability of organisms to maintain internal stability (pH, temperature, glucose, water balance). Human blood glucose: after eating, the pancreas releases insulin (lowers glucose); fasting triggers glucagon (raises glucose). This negative feedback system keeps glucose at ~5 mmol/L.

questions.question: What are the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis?
choices: The reactions that occur in the stroma using ATP and NADPH to fix CO₂
Reactions occurring in the thylakoid membranes that use light energy to produce ATP, NADPH, and release O₂ through water splitting
The reactions that convert glucose to pyruvate in the cytoplasm
Reactions using chlorophyll to directly convert CO₂ into glucose
questions.answer: Reactions occurring in the thylakoid membranes that use light energy to produce ATP, NADPH, and release O₂ through water splitting
questions.explanation: Light-dependent reactions (light reactions) occur in the thylakoid membranes. Light excites electrons in chlorophyll → electron transport chain → produces ATP (photophosphorylation) and NADPH. Water molecules are split (photolysis), releasing O₂ as a byproduct. Products (ATP and NADPH) power the Calvin Cycle.

questions.question: What is the Calvin cycle?
choices: The process of breaking down glucose to produce ATP in mitochondria
The light-independent reactions in the chloroplast stroma that use ATP and NADPH to convert CO₂ into glucose
The process by which amino acids are assembled into proteins in ribosomes
The cycle of nitrogen through the soil, atmosphere, and plants
questions.answer: The light-independent reactions in the chloroplast stroma that use ATP and NADPH to convert CO₂ into glucose
questions.explanation: The Calvin Cycle (C3 photosynthesis) occurs in the stroma and uses CO₂, ATP, and NADPH to produce G3P (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate), from which glucose is synthesized. The enzyme RuBisCO catalyzes CO₂ fixation. Three turns of the cycle produce one net G3P.

questions.question: What is a keystone species?
choices: The most numerous species in an ecosystem
A species that has a disproportionately large effect on ecosystem structure relative to its abundance
The largest predator at the top of a food chain
A species found exclusively in a single geographic location
questions.answer: A species that has a disproportionately large effect on ecosystem structure relative to its abundance
questions.explanation: A keystone species has an ecological impact far exceeding its biomass. Classic example: sea otters control sea urchin populations, which otherwise overgraze kelp forests. Removing a keystone species causes dramatic ecosystem change (trophic cascade). Sea stars (Paine's experiments) and wolves (Yellowstone) are other examples.

questions.question: What is the difference between innate and adaptive immunity?
choices: Innate immunity is stronger; adaptive immunity is faster
Innate immunity is immediate, non-specific, and present from birth; adaptive immunity is specific, has memory, and involves T and B lymphocytes
Innate immunity involves antibodies; adaptive immunity involves complement proteins
Innate immunity learns from infections; adaptive immunity is fixed from birth
questions.answer: Innate immunity is immediate, non-specific, and present from birth; adaptive immunity is specific, has memory, and involves T and B lymphocytes
questions.explanation: Innate immunity (physical barriers, phagocytes, NK cells, inflammation, complement) is non-specific and responds immediately. Adaptive immunity (T cells, B cells, antibodies) is antigen-specific, develops over days, and creates immunological memory — the basis for vaccination.

questions.question: What is the sliding filament theory of muscle contraction?
choices: Muscle cells physically increase in length when contracted
Actin (thin) filaments slide over myosin (thick) filaments, shortening the sarcomere without the filaments themselves changing length
Myosin filaments break down and reform to generate contractile force
Calcium ions directly provide the energy for muscle contraction
questions.answer: Actin (thin) filaments slide over myosin (thick) filaments, shortening the sarcomere without the filaments themselves changing length
questions.explanation: The sliding filament theory (Huxley & Hanson, 1954) explains muscle contraction: myosin heads bind to actin, pull it toward the center of the sarcomere (power stroke), then release and reset. ATP provides energy; calcium (from SR) triggers contraction by exposing myosin binding sites on actin.

questions.question: What is polyploidy and its significance in plant evolution?
choices: Having more than the normal number of chromosomes due to non-disjunction in meiosis
Having more than two complete sets of chromosomes (3n, 4n, etc.) — a major mechanism of speciation in plants
A condition where multiple genes control a single trait
The presence of multiple alleles for a gene in a population
questions.answer: Having more than two complete sets of chromosomes (3n, 4n, etc.) — a major mechanism of speciation in plants
questions.explanation: Polyploidy (having >2 sets of chromosomes) is much more common in plants than animals and is a major driver of plant speciation. Many crop plants are polyploid (wheat is hexaploid-6n; potato is tetraploid-4n; banana is triploid-3n). Allopolyploidy (combining genomes from different species) can create new fertile species instantly.

questions.question: What is the nitrogen cycle and why is it important?
choices: The movement of nitrogen through the food web from producers to consumers
The biogeochemical cycle transforming nitrogen between atmospheric N₂, soil nitrates/ammonium, and organic compounds — essential for amino acid and nucleotide synthesis
The process by which plants absorb nitrogen from the atmosphere directly
The chemical conversion of nitrogen to oxygen in photosynthesis
questions.answer: The biogeochemical cycle transforming nitrogen between atmospheric N₂, soil nitrates/ammonium, and organic compounds — essential for amino acid and nucleotide synthesis
questions.explanation: The nitrogen cycle: N₂ fixation (Rhizobium, lightning) → ammonification (decomposers) → nitrification (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) → plant uptake → consumption → denitrification (Pseudomonas) → N₂. Since N₂ cannot be used directly by most organisms, biological nitrogen fixation is crucial for ecosystem productivity.

questions.question: What is population ecology's concept of carrying capacity (K)?
choices: The initial population size before any growth occurs
The maximum population size a given environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources
The rate at which a population grows under ideal conditions
The minimum viable population needed to avoid extinction
questions.answer: The maximum population size a given environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is determined by limiting factors (food, water, shelter, space, disease). Logistic population growth models use K: dN/dt = rN(1-N/K). As N approaches K, growth slows and stops. Population regulation mechanisms (density-dependent factors) become stronger as K is approached.

questions.question: What is epistasis?
choices: When two alleles of the same gene are both expressed (codominance)
When the expression of one gene is masked or modified by a different gene at another locus
When multiple genes contribute to a single quantitative trait (polygenic inheritance)
When a single gene affects multiple phenotypic traits (pleiotropy)
questions.answer: When the expression of one gene is masked or modified by a different gene at another locus
questions.explanation: Epistasis is gene-gene interaction where one gene (epistatic gene) masks or modifies the phenotypic effect of another gene (hypostatic gene) at a different locus. Classic example: coat color in Labrador dogs — a second gene determines whether pigment is deposited in the fur (B/b) or not (E/e gene acts epistatically).

questions.question: What is horizontal gene transfer (HGT) and why is it significant in microbiology?
choices: Transfer of genes from parent to offspring through sexual reproduction
Transfer of genetic material between organisms outside the normal parent-to-offspring pathway — major mechanism of antibiotic resistance spread in bacteria
Transfer of genes between cells of the same organism during development
The movement of transposons within a single organism's genome
questions.answer: Transfer of genetic material between organisms outside the normal parent-to-offspring pathway — major mechanism of antibiotic resistance spread in bacteria
questions.explanation: HGT in bacteria occurs through: (1) transformation (uptake of free DNA), (2) transduction (via phage), and (3) conjugation (direct cell contact via pili). HGT allows bacteria to rapidly acquire antibiotic resistance genes, making it a major public health challenge.

questions.question: What is a biome and how are biomes classified?
choices: A single ecosystem in a geographic location
A large-scale community of organisms defined primarily by climate (temperature and precipitation) and characterized by distinctive vegetation
A classification of organisms based on their ecological roles
The total diversity of species in a given geographic region
questions.answer: A large-scale community of organisms defined primarily by climate (temperature and precipitation) and characterized by distinctive vegetation
questions.explanation: Biomes are major terrestrial communities defined by climate. Types include: tropical rainforest, savanna, desert, temperate deciduous forest, temperate grassland, taiga (boreal forest), and tundra. The Philippines falls primarily in the tropical rainforest biome, characterized by high biodiversity and year-round warmth.

questions.question: What is the difference between incomplete dominance and codominance?
choices: They are identical phenomena — the heterozygote shows both parental phenotypes
Incomplete dominance produces a blended intermediate phenotype; codominance shows both parental phenotypes simultaneously in the same organism
Incomplete dominance occurs only in flowers; codominance occurs only in blood types
Incomplete dominance results in three phenotypes; codominance results in only two
questions.answer: Incomplete dominance produces a blended intermediate phenotype; codominance shows both parental phenotypes simultaneously in the same organism
questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance: RR (red) × WW (white) → RW (pink) — a blend. Codominance: both alleles are fully expressed simultaneously — IAIB blood type shows both A and B antigens, human hemoglobin in sickle cell trait shows both normal and sickle hemoglobin.

questions.question: What is the role of ribosomes in cells?
choices: Digesting cellular waste and foreign particles
Synthesizing proteins by translating mRNA sequences according to the genetic code
Packaging proteins for export from the cell
Storing lipids for cellular membrane production
questions.answer: Synthesizing proteins by translating mRNA sequences according to the genetic code
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis. They read mRNA codons (3-nucleotide sequences) and recruit corresponding aminoacyl-tRNA molecules to assemble the polypeptide chain. Free ribosomes synthesize cytoplasmic proteins; ribosomes on the rough ER synthesize membrane and secretory proteins.

questions.question: What is a phylogenetic tree and what does it show?
choices: A map showing the geographic distribution of related species
A branching diagram representing the evolutionary relationships and common ancestors among groups of organisms
A family tree showing the inheritance of traits within a single species
A classification system for dividing species into taxonomic groups
questions.answer: A branching diagram representing the evolutionary relationships and common ancestors among groups of organisms
questions.explanation: A phylogenetic tree (cladogram) uses morphological, molecular, and genetic data to infer evolutionary history. Branch points (nodes) represent common ancestors; branch lengths may represent time or genetic distance. Modern phylogenetics relies heavily on DNA and rRNA sequence comparisons.

questions.question: What is the Hardy-Weinberg principle and what are its five equilibrium conditions?
choices: A principle stating that evolution always occurs at a constant rate in large populations
A mathematical model stating allele frequencies remain constant if no evolution is occurring; requires no mutation, random mating, large population, no gene flow, and no natural selection
A principle explaining how new alleles arise through mutation in small populations
A model showing that dominant alleles always increase in frequency over time
questions.answer: A mathematical model stating allele frequencies remain constant if no evolution is occurring; requires no mutation, random mating, large population, no gene flow, and no natural selection
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium: p² + 2pq + q² = 1 (where p and q are allele frequencies). A population in HWE is not evolving. Any violation of the 5 conditions (no mutation, random mating, large population, no migration, no selection) causes allele frequencies to change — i.e., evolution is occurring.

questions.question: What is active transport and how does it differ from passive transport?
choices: Active transport moves molecules from low to high concentration, requires ATP; passive transport moves molecules down their concentration gradient without energy
Active transport uses carrier proteins; passive transport occurs through the lipid bilayer only
Active transport is faster; passive transport is slower
Active transport occurs only in plant cells; passive transport occurs in all cells
questions.answer: Active transport moves molecules from low to high concentration, requires ATP; passive transport moves molecules down their concentration gradient without energy
questions.explanation: Passive transport (diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis) moves molecules down concentration gradients — no energy required. Active transport moves molecules against their gradient using ATP (e.g., Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump, proton pump). It is essential for maintaining cellular ion gradients and nutrient absorption.

questions.question: What is carrying capacity in population ecology and what factors determine it?
choices: The minimum population size for genetic viability
The maximum population size an environment can sustain, determined by limiting factors like food, water, space, and disease
The rate of population growth under ideal conditions
The number of individuals per unit area at any given time
questions.answer: The maximum population size an environment can sustain, determined by limiting factors like food, water, space, and disease
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the population size at which birth rate equals death rate (population growth = 0). It is set by limiting resources: food, water, shelter, mates, and disease. Density-dependent factors (competition, predation, disease) become stronger as the population approaches K, regulating growth.

questions.question: What is the role of the liver in human digestion?
choices: Producing digestive enzymes that break down proteins in the stomach
Producing bile salts that emulsify fats, and processing absorbed nutrients from the portal circulation
Absorbing water and electrolytes from digested food
Secreting insulin to regulate blood glucose after meals
questions.answer: Producing bile salts that emulsify fats, and processing absorbed nutrients from the portal circulation
questions.explanation: The liver: (1) produces bile (stored in gallbladder) containing bile salts that emulsify dietary fats for lipase action; (2) receives portal blood with absorbed nutrients and processes them; (3) performs gluconeogenesis, glycogen storage, protein synthesis, and detoxification.

questions.question: What is lateral meristem and what is its function in plants?
choices: Tissue at shoot and root tips responsible for primary growth (increase in length)
Tissue on the sides of stems and roots responsible for secondary growth (increase in girth/width)
Tissue responsible for producing flowers and reproductive structures
Tissue at leaf margins that produces lateral branches
questions.answer: Tissue on the sides of stems and roots responsible for secondary growth (increase in girth/width)
questions.explanation: Lateral meristems are the vascular cambium (produces secondary xylem and phloem — wood and bark) and cork cambium (produces periderm). They are responsible for secondary growth — increasing the diameter of stems and roots. Only dicots and gymnosperms have significant lateral meristem activity.

questions.question: What is the role of the kidney in homeostasis?
choices: Producing insulin to regulate blood glucose
Filtering blood to remove metabolic wastes, regulating water and electrolyte balance, and maintaining blood pH
Producing bile to aid in fat digestion
Synthesizing red blood cells for oxygen transport
questions.answer: Filtering blood to remove metabolic wastes, regulating water and electrolyte balance, and maintaining blood pH
questions.explanation: The kidneys perform: (1) filtration of ~180L of blood plasma/day; (2) selective reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, water, ions; (3) secretion of waste products (urea, creatinine, drugs); (4) regulation of blood volume and pressure (renin-angiotensin system); (5) pH regulation through bicarbonate control.

questions.question: What is transpiration in plants and what factors affect its rate?
choices: The process of absorbing water through root hairs from the soil
The loss of water vapor through stomata, primarily from leaves, driven by temperature, humidity, wind, and light
The transport of sugars from leaves to roots through the phloem
The process of CO₂ entering leaves through stomata during photosynthesis
questions.answer: The loss of water vapor through stomata, primarily from leaves, driven by temperature, humidity, wind, and light
questions.explanation: Transpiration pulls water up through the xylem (transpiration pull/cohesion-tension mechanism). Factors increasing transpiration: higher temperature, lower humidity, wind, higher light intensity (opens stomata). Abscisic acid (ABA) causes stomatal closure during drought. 97% of water absorbed by roots is transpired.

questions.question: What is the difference between a virus and a bacterium?
choices: Viruses are larger than bacteria
Viruses are non-cellular obligate intracellular parasites requiring a host to replicate; bacteria are single-celled organisms capable of independent reproduction
Both are treated effectively with antibiotics
Viruses have a cell wall; bacteria do not
questions.answer: Viruses are non-cellular obligate intracellular parasites requiring a host to replicate; bacteria are single-celled organisms capable of independent reproduction
questions.explanation: Bacteria: prokaryotic cells with their own ribosomes, metabolic machinery, and ability to reproduce independently. Can be killed by antibiotics. Viruses: acellular (protein coat + nucleic acid), no ribosomes or independent metabolism, require host cell machinery to replicate. Antibiotics are ineffective against viruses.

questions.question: What is ecological succession and what is the difference between primary and secondary succession?
choices: The evolution of species over geological time scales
The directional change in community composition over time; primary begins on bare substrate with no soil; secondary begins where soil remains after disturbance
The replacement of one ecosystem type by another due to climate change
The seasonal changes in species abundance throughout the year
questions.answer: The directional change in community composition over time; primary begins on bare substrate with no soil; secondary begins where soil remains after disturbance
questions.explanation: Ecological succession is the process of community change over time. Primary succession (e.g., on volcanic rock or glacial till) begins with pioneer species (lichens, mosses) and can take centuries. Secondary succession (e.g., after forest fire, agricultural abandonment) is faster because soil and seed banks remain.

questions.question: What is the significance of the phospholipid bilayer structure of the cell membrane?
choices: It provides rigid structural support for the cell
Its amphipathic nature (hydrophilic heads outward, hydrophobic tails inward) creates a selectively permeable barrier that controls the passage of substances
It stores energy in the form of phospholipid molecules
It facilitates all active transport across the membrane through lipid channels
questions.answer: Its amphipathic nature (hydrophilic heads outward, hydrophobic tails inward) creates a selectively permeable barrier that controls the passage of substances
questions.explanation: Phospholipids have hydrophilic (water-loving) phosphate heads and hydrophobic (water-fearing) fatty acid tails. In water, they spontaneously form bilayers with tails facing inward. This creates a barrier that: freely allows nonpolar/small molecules through, restricts ions and large polar molecules, requiring transport proteins.

questions.question: What is gene expression and how is it regulated in eukaryotes?
choices: Gene expression refers only to translation of mRNA into proteins

Gene expression is the process by which genetic information produces a functional product (RNA or protein), regulated at transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, and post-translational levels
Gene expression is the physical appearance of genetic traits in an organism
Gene expression occurs only when genes are permanently activated
questions.answer: Gene expression is the process by which genetic information produces a functional product (RNA or protein), regulated at transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, and post-translational levels
questions.explanation: Gene expression encompasses both transcription (DNA → mRNA) and translation (mRNA → protein). Eukaryotic regulation includes: transcriptional (promoters, enhancers, transcription factors, chromatin remodeling); post-transcriptional (splicing, miRNA); translational (ribosome access); post-translational (protein modification, degradation).
